166→
167→    /**
168→     * Adds a single provider of an additional rule to be executed when linting/formatting the code. This can to be used
169→     * to unit test rules which are best to be tested in conjunction with other rules, for example wrapping and
170→     * indenting.
171→     *
172→     * Caution:
173→     * An additional rule provider for a rule which actually is executed before the rule under test, might result in
174→     * unexpected [LintViolation] in case that additional rule is modifying the AST. During the [lint] phase, all rules
175→     * are executed in parallel and as of that both the rule under test and the additional rule are using the exact same
176→     * version of the AST. When, in the [format] stage the additional rule is run first, and if it modifies the AST,
177→     * this could result in a [LintViolation] for the rule under test which is executed later than the additional rule.
178→     *
179→     * Prefer to use [addAdditionalRuleProviders] when adding multiple providers of rules.
180→     */
181→    public fun addAdditionalRuleProvider(provider: () -> Rule): KtLintAssertThat {
182→        additionalRuleProviders.add(RuleProvider(provider))
183→
184→        return this
185→    }
186→
187→    /**
188→     * Adds a single provider of an additional rule to be executed when linting/formatting the code. This can to be used
189→     * to unit test rules which are best to be tested in conjunction with other rules, for example wrapping and
190→     * indenting.
191→     *
192→     * Caution:
193→     * An additional rule provider for a rule which actually is executed before the rule under test, might result in
194→     * unexpected [LintViolation] in case that additional rule is modifying the AST. During the [lint] phase, all rules
195→     * are executed in parallel and as of that both the rule under test and the additional rule are using the exact same
196→     * version of the AST. When, in the [format] stage the additional rule is run first, and if it modifies the AST,
197→     * this could result in a [LintViolation] for the rule under test which is executed later than the additional rule.
198→     *
199→     * Prefer to use [addAdditionalRuleProvider] when only a singe provider of a rule is to be added.
200→     */
201→    public fun addAdditionalRuleProviders(vararg providers: (() -> Rule)): KtLintAssertThat {
202→        additionalRuleProviders.addAll(
203→            providers.map { RuleProvider(it) },
204→        )
205→
206→        return this
207→    }
208→
209→    /**
210→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s in the rule associated with the KtLintAssertThat.
211→     *
212→     * Note: When linting succeeds without errors, formatting is also checked.
213→     */
214→    public fun hasNoLintViolations(): Unit = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasNoLintViolations()
215→
216→    /**
217→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s for a given rule id.
218→     */
219→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsForRuleId(ruleId: RuleId): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
220→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasNoLintViolationsForRuleId(ruleId)
221→
222→    /**
223→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s except in the additional formatting rules.
224→     *
225→     * Note: When linting succeeds without errors, formatting is also checked.
226→     */
227→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules(): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
228→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules()
229→
230→    /**
231→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] which automatically can be corrected. This is a sugar
232→     * coated version of [hasLintViolations] for the case that the code contains exactly one lint violation.
233→     */
234→    public fun hasLintViolation(
235→        line: Int,
236→        col: Int,
237→        detail: String,
238→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolation(line, col, detail)
239→
240→    /**
241→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s which can be automatically corrected. Note that tests
242→     * resulting in only one (type of) [LintViolation] are usually easier to comprehend.
243→     */
244→    public fun hasLintViolations(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
245→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolations(*expectedErrors)
246→
247→    /**
248→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] caused by an additional rule which automatically can be
249→     * corrected. This is a sugar coated version of [hasLintViolations] for the case that the code contains exactly one
250→     * lint violation.
251→     */
252→    public fun hasLintViolationForAdditionalRule(
253→        line: Int,
254→        col: Int,
255→        detail: String,
256→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolationForAdditionalRule(line, col, detail)
257→
258→    /**
259→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s caused by an additional rules which can be
260→     * automatically corrected. Note that tests resulting in only one (type of) [LintViolation] are usually easier to
261→     * comprehend.
262→     */
263→    public fun hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRule(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
264→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules(*expectedErrors)
265→
266→    /**
267→     * Asserts that the code is formatted as given.
268→     */
269→    public fun isFormattedAs(formattedCode: String): KtLintAssertThatAssertable = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().isFormattedAs(formattedCode)
270→
271→    /**
272→     * Asserts that the code does contain the given [LintViolation] which can not be automatically corrected.
273→     */
274→    public fun hasLintViolationWithoutAutoCorrect(
275→        line: Int,
276→        col: Int,
277→        detail: String,
278→    ): Unit = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolationWithoutAutoCorrect(line, col, detail)
279→
280→    /**
281→     * Asserts that the code does contain the given [LintViolation]s which can not be automatically corrected. Note that
282→     * tests resulting in only one [LintViolation] are usually easier to comprehend.
283→     */
284→    public fun hasLintViolationsWithoutAutoCorrect(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): Unit =
285→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolationsWithoutAutocorrect(*expectedErrors)
286→
287→    private fun ktLintAssertThatAssertable(): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
288→        KtLintAssertThatAssertable(
289→            ruleProvider = ruleProvider,
290→            code = code(),
291→            editorConfigOverride = editorConfigProperties.toEditorConfigOverride(),
292→            additionalRuleProviders = additionalRuleProviders.toSet(),
293→        )
294→
295→    private fun MutableSet<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>>.toEditorConfigOverride() =
296→        if (isEmpty()) {
297→            EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_OVERRIDE
298→        } else {
299→            EditorConfigOverride.from(*toTypedArray())
300→        }
301→
302→    private fun code() =
303→        if (filePath != null) {
304→            // Create a file containing the code and the given filepath. CodeFile can not be used here as it can not read successfully from
305→            // the Ktlint test file system.
306→            val path = KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM.resolve(filePath)
307→            KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM.writeFile(path.pathString, code)
308→            Code.fromPath(path)
309→        } else {
310→            Code.fromSnippet(code, kotlinScript)
311→        }
312→
313→    public companion object {
314→        /**
315→         * Creates an assertThat assertion function for the rule provided by [provider]. This assertion function has
316→         * extensions specifically for testing KtLint rules.
317→         */
318→        public fun assertThatRule(provider: () -> Rule): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat = RuleProvider { provider() }.assertThat()
319→
320→        /**
321→         * Creates an assertThat assertion function for the rule provided by [provider]. This assertion function has
322→         * extensions specifically for testing KtLint rules. The rules provided via [additionalRuleProviders] are only
323→         * executed during the format phase of the test. This means that the unit test only has to check the lint
324→         * violations thrown by the rule for which the assertThat is created. But the code is formatted by both the rule
325→         * and the rules provided by [additionalRuleProviders] in the order as defined by the rule definitions.
326→         */
327→
328→        public fun assertThatRule(
329→            provider: () -> Rule,
330→            additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider> = emptySet(),
331→            editorConfigProperties: Set<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>> = emptySet(),
332→        ): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat = RuleProvider { provider() }.assertThat(additionalRuleProviders, editorConfigProperties)
333→
334→        private fun RuleProvider.assertThat(
335→            additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider> = emptySet(),
336→            editorConfigProperties: Set<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>> = emptySet(),
337→        ): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat =
338→            { code ->
339→                KtLintAssertThat(
340→                    ruleProvider = this,
341→                    code = code,
342→                    additionalRuleProviders = additionalRuleProviders.toMutableSet(),
343→                    editorConfigProperties = editorConfigProperties.toMutableSet(),
344→                )
345→            }
346→
347→        /**
348→         * See [setMaxLineLength] for intended usage.
349→         */
350→        public const val MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER: String = "Max line length marker:"
351→
352→        /**
353→         * See [setMaxLineLength] for intended usage.
354→         */
355→        public const val EOL_CHAR: Char = '#'
356→
357→        init {
358→            /* A unit test using the max line length marker typically looks like:
359→             * val code =
360→             *     """
361→             *     // $MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER                   $EOL_CHAR
362→             *     val fooooooooooooooo = "fooooooooooooooooooooo"
363→             *     """.trimIndent()
364→             * In order for this to work properly, the length of "$MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER" must be identical to the length of the real value
365→             * of the constant MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.
366→             */
367→            require("$".plus(::MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.name).length == MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.length) {
368→                "Length of the value of the string '${'$'}MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER' must be identical to length of the value of the " +
369→                    "constant. Tests using this constant will not work as intended otherwise."
370→            }
371→        }
372→    }
373→}
374→
375→/**
376→ * Immutable assertable. Once the first assertion is made on [KtLintAssertThat] it is converted to the
377→ * [KtLintAssertThatAssertable] which allows no further modifications of the internal state. This guarantees that all
378→ * assertions operate on the same state.
379→ */
380→public class KtLintAssertThatAssertable(
381→    /** The provider of the rule which is the subject of the test, e.g. the rule for which the AssertThat is created. */
382→    private val ruleProvider: RuleProvider,
383→    private val code: Code,
384→    private val editorConfigOverride: EditorConfigOverride = EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_OVERRIDE,
385→    /**
386→     *  The rules which have to be executed in addition to the main rule when linting/formatting the code. Note that
387→     *  lint errors for those rules are suppressed.
388→     */
389→    private val additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider>,
390→) : AbstractAssert<KtLintAssertThatAssertable, String>(code.content, KtLintAssertThatAssertable::class.java) {
391→    private val ruleId = ruleProvider.createNewRuleInstance().ruleId
392→
393→    /**
394→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s caused by the rule associated with the
395→     * KtLintAssertThat.
396→     *
397→     * Note: When linting succeeds without errors, formatting is also checked.
398→     */
399→    public fun hasNoLintViolations() {
400→        assertThat(lint().filterCurrentRuleOnly()).isEmpty()
401→
402→        // Also format the code to be absolutely sure that codes does not get changed
403→        val (actualFormattedCode, lintErrorsWhenFormatting) = format()
404→
405→        assertAll(
406→            {
407→                assertThat(lintErrorsWhenFormatting)
408→                    .describedAs(
409→                        "LintViolations found by format while no lint errors were found during Lint. " +
410→                            if (additionalRuleProviders.isEmpty()) {
411→                                "This is unexpected as no additional rule have been defined for this test."
412→                            } else {
413→                                "If this is caused by an additional rule added to the test, then remove that rule in case it " +
414→                                    "is always executed before the rule under test."
415→                            },
416→                    )
417→                    .isEmpty()
418→            },
419→            {
420→                assertThat(actualFormattedCode)
421→                    .describedAs(
422→                        "Code is changed by format while no lint errors were found. " +
423→                            if (additionalRuleProviders.isEmpty()) {
424→                                "This is unexpected as no additional rule have been defined for this test."
425→                            } else {
426→                                "If this is caused by an additional rule added to the test, then remove that rule in case it " +
427→                                    "is always executed before the rule under test."
428→                            },
429→                    )
430→                    .isEqualTo(code.content)
431→            },
432→        )
433→    }
434→
435→    /**
436→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s for the given rule id.
437→     */
438→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsForRuleId(ruleId: RuleId): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
439→        val (_, lintErrorsWhenFormatting) = format()
440→
441→        assertThat(lintErrorsWhenFormatting.filter { it.ruleId == ruleId })
442→            .describedAs("At least 1 lint violation was found for rule id '${ruleId.value}' while none were expected")
443→            .isEmpty()
444→
445→        return this
446→    }
447→
448→    /**
449→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s except in the additional formatting rules.
450→     *
451→     * Note that this method can and should be chained with [isFormattedAs] to verify whether the code is correctly
452→     * formatted.
453→     *
454→     * This method can be used when the rule which is associated with the KtLintAssertThat is not violated by the sample
455→     * code, but the code is reformatted by the additional formatting rules. In case that rules are dependent on each
456→     * other, a unit test cal still verify that code is formatted correctly even when the rule under test is not
457→     * violated.
458→     */
459→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules(): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
460→        check(additionalRuleProviders.isNotEmpty()) {
461→            "hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules can only be used when additional rules have been added"
462→        }
463→
464→        val lintErrors = lint()
465→        assertThat(lintErrors.filterCurrentRuleOnly()).isEmpty()
466→        assertThat(lintErrors.filterAdditionalRulesOnly()).isNotEmpty
467→
468→        return this
469→    }
470→
471→    /**
472→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]. This is a sugar coated version of
473→     * [hasLintViolation] for the case that the code contains exactly one lint violation.
474→     */
475→    public fun hasLintViolation(
476→        line: Int,
477→        col: Int,
478→        detail: String,
479→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
480→        hasLintViolations(
481→            LintViolation(
482→                line = line,
483→                col = col,
484→                detail = detail,
485→            ),
486→        )
487→
488→    /**
489→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] caused by an additional rule. This is a sugar coated
490→     * version of [hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules] for the case that the code contains exactly one lint violation.
491→     */
492→    public fun hasLintViolationForAdditionalRule(
493→        line: Int,
494→        col: Int,
495→        detail: String,
496→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
497→        hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules(
498→            LintViolation(
499→                line = line,
500→                col = col,
501→                detail = detail,
502→            ),
503→        )
504→
505→    /**
506→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s which can be automatically corrected.
507→     */
508→    public fun hasLintViolations(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
509→        check(expectedErrors.isNotEmpty())
510→
511→        val actualLintViolationFields =
512→            lint()
513→                .filterCurrentRuleOnly()
514→                .toLintViolationsFields()
515→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
516→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can be automatically corrected")
517→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedErrors.toLintViolationsFields(canBeAutoCorrected = true))
518→        return this
519→    }
520→
521→    /**
522→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s caused by additional rules and which can be
523→     * automatically corrected.
524→     */
525→    public fun hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
526→        check(expectedErrors.isNotEmpty())
527→
528→        val actualLintViolationFields =
529→            lint()
530→                .filterAdditionalRulesOnly()
531→                .toLintViolationsFields()
532→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
533→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can be automatically corrected")
534→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedErrors.toLintViolationsFields(canBeAutoCorrected = true))
535→        return this
536→    }
537→
538→    /**
539→     * Asserts that the code is formatted as given.
540→     */
541→    public fun isFormattedAs(formattedCode: String): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
542→        check(formattedCode != code.content) {
543→            "Use '.hasNoLintViolations()' instead of '.isFormattedAs(<original code>)'"
544→        }
545→
546→        // Also format the code to be absolutely sure that codes does not get changed
547→        val (actualFormattedCode, _) = format()
548→
549→        assertThat(actualFormattedCode)
550→            .describedAs("Code is formatted as")
551→            .isEqualTo(formattedCode)
552→
553→        return this
554→    }
555→
556→    /**
557→     * Asserts that the code contains the given [LintViolation] which can not be automatically corrected.
558→     */
559→    public fun hasLintViolationWithoutAutoCorrect(
560→        line: Int,
561→        col: Int,
562→        detail: String,
563→    ): Unit =
564→        hasLintViolationsWithoutAutocorrect(
565→            LintViolation(
566→                line = line,
567→                col = col,
568→                detail = detail,
569→            ),
570→        )
571→
572→    /**
573→     * Asserts that the code does contain the given [LintViolation]s and that those violations can not be automatically
574→     * corrected.
575→     */
576→    public fun hasLintViolationsWithoutAutocorrect(vararg expectedLintViolations: LintViolation) {
577→        check(expectedLintViolations.isNotEmpty())
578→
579→        val actualLintViolationFields =
580→            lint()
581→                .filterCurrentRuleOnly()
582→                .toLintViolationsFields()
583→
584→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
585→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can not be automatically corrected")
586→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedLintViolations.toLintViolationsFields(canBeAutoCorrected = false))
587→    }
588→
589→    private fun Array<out LintViolation>.toLintViolationsFields(canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean): Array<LintViolationFields> {
590→        return map {
591→            LintViolationFields(
592→                line = it.line,
593→                col = it.col,
594→                detail = it.detail,
595→                canBeAutoCorrected = canBeAutoCorrected,
596→            )
597→        }.toTypedArray()
598→    }
599→
600→    private fun List<LintError>.filterAdditionalRulesOnly() = filter { it.ruleId != ruleId }
601→
602→    private fun List<LintError>.filterCurrentRuleOnly() = filter { it.ruleId == ruleId }
603→
604→    private fun List<LintError>.toLintViolationsFields(): Array<LintViolationFields> =
605→        map {
606→            LintViolationFields(
607→                line = it.line,
608→                col = it.col,
609→                detail = it.detail,
610→                canBeAutoCorrected = it.canBeAutoCorrected,
611→            )
612→        }.toTypedArray()
613→
614→    private fun createKtLintRuleEngine(): KtLintRuleEngine {
615→        val ruleProviders =
616→            setOf(ruleProvider)
617→                // Also run the additional rules as the main rule might have a VisitorModifier which requires one or more of
618→                // the additional rules to be loaded and enabled as well.
619→                .plus(additionalRuleProviders)
620→                .plus(DUMP_AST_RULE_PROVIDER)
621→        val editorConfigOverride =
622→            editorConfigOverride
623→                .enableExperimentalRules()
624→                .extendWithRuleSetRuleExecutionsFor(ruleProviders)
625→        return KtLintRuleEngine(
626→            ruleProviders = ruleProviders,
627→            editorConfigOverride = editorConfigOverride,
628→            fileSystem = KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM.fileSystem,
629→        )
630→    }
631→
632→    private fun lint(): List<LintError> {
633→        val lintErrors = ArrayList<LintError>()
634→        createKtLintRuleEngine().lint(code) { lintError -> lintErrors.add(lintError) }
635→        return lintErrors
636→    }
637→
638→    private fun format(): Pair<String, List<LintError>> {
639→        val lintErrors = ArrayList<LintError>()
640→        val formattedCode = createKtLintRuleEngine().format(code) { lintError, _ -> lintErrors.add(lintError) }
641→        return Pair(formattedCode, lintErrors)
642→    }
643→
644→    /* Representation of the field of the [LintError] that should be identical. Note that no comparison can be made
645→     * against the original [LintError] as the [canBeAutoCorrected] flag is excluded from the hashcode.
646→     */
647→    private data class LintViolationFields(
648→        val line: Int,
649→        val col: Int,
650→        val detail: String,
651→        val canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean,
652→    )
653→}
654→
655→internal class MissingEolMarker : RuntimeException(
656→    """
657→    The first line of the provide code sample should contain text '$MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER' which is provided by the
658→    constant '${::MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.name}' and ends with the EOL_CHAR'$EOL_CHAR' provided by the constant
659→    '${::EOL_CHAR.name}' which indicates the last position that is allowed.
660→    """.trimIndent(),
661→)
662→
663→/**
664→ * Expectation of the [LintError]. Contrary to the [LintError] it does not contain the ruleId. The ruleId will be
665→ * derived from the rule for which the AssertThat was created.
666→ */
667→public data class LintViolation(
668→    val line: Int,
669→    val col: Int,
670→    val detail: String,
671→)
672→
673→/**
674→ * Enables the rule sets for the given set of [ruleProviders] unless the rule execution of that rule set was already
675→ * provided.
676→ */
677→private fun EditorConfigOverride.extendWithRuleSetRuleExecutionsFor(ruleProviders: Set<RuleProvider>): EditorConfigOverride {
678→    val ruleSetRuleExecutions =
679→        ruleProviders
680→            .asSequence()
681→            .map { ruleProvider ->
682→                ruleProvider
683→                    .createNewRuleInstance()
684→                    .ruleId
685→                    .ruleSetId
686→                    .createRuleSetExecutionEditorConfigProperty()
687→            }.distinct()
688→            .filter { editorConfigProperty -> this.properties[editorConfigProperty] == null }
689→            .map { it to RuleExecution.enabled }
690→            .toList()
691→            .toTypedArray()
692→    return this.plus(*ruleSetRuleExecutions)
693→}
694→
695→private fun EditorConfigOverride.enableExperimentalRules(): EditorConfigOverride =
696→    plus(EXPERIMENTAL_RULES_EXECUTION_PROPERTY to RuleExecution.enabled)

 17→     * To use the [IndentConfig] in a rule, the following needs to be done:
 18→     * 1. Implement interface [UsesEditorConfigProperties] on the rule
 19→     * 2. Register the used or properties
 20→     *    ```
 21→     *    override val editorConfigProperties: List<UsesEditorConfigProperties.EditorConfigProperty<*>> =
 22→     *        listOf(indentSizeProperty, indentStyleProperty)
 23→     *    ```
 24→     * 3. Initialize the IndentConfig
 25→     *    ```
 26→     *    indentConfig = IndentConfig(
 27→     *        indentStyle = node.getEditorConfigValue(indentStyleProperty),
 28→     *        tabWidth = node.getEditorConfigValue(indentSizeProperty)
 29→     *    )
 30→     *    ```
 31→     */
 32→    public constructor(
 33→        indentStyle: PropertyType.IndentStyleValue,
 34→        /**
 35→         * The number of spaces that is equivalent to one tab
 36→         */
 37→        tabWidth: Int,
 38→    ) : this(
 39→        indentStyle =
 40→            when (indentStyle) {
 41→                PropertyType.IndentStyleValue.tab -> TAB
 42→                PropertyType.IndentStyleValue.space -> SPACE
 43→            },
 44→        tabWidth = tabWidth,
 45→    )
 46→
 47→    public enum class IndentStyle { SPACE, TAB }
 48→
 49→    private val indentChar =
 50→        when (indentStyle) {
 51→            TAB -> '\t'
 52→            SPACE -> ' '
 53→        }
 54→
 55→    private val unexpectedIndentChar =
 56→        when (indentStyle) {
 57→            TAB -> ' '
 58→            SPACE -> '\t'
 59→        }
 60→
 61→    /**
 62→     * When disabled, rules may not enforce any indentation related changes regardless of other settings in this
 63→     * configuration.
 64→     */
 65→    public val disabled: Boolean
 66→        get() = tabWidth <= 0
 67→
 68→    /**
 69→     * Normalized indent of 1 level deep. Representation is either in spaces or a single tab depending on the
 70→     * configuration.
 71→     */
 72→    public val indent: String =
 73→        if (disabled) {
 74→            ""
 75→        } else {
 76→            when (indentStyle) {
 77→                TAB -> indentChar.toString()
 78→                SPACE -> indentChar.toString().repeat(tabWidth)
 79→            }
 80→        }
 81→
 82→    /**
 83→     * Converts [text] to a normalized indent. If [text] contains a new line, then only text after the last new line
 84→     * is converted. This text may only contain spaces and tabs.
 85→     *
 86→     * Tabs in the given text are converted to spaces depending on the tab size of the indent style.
 87→     *
 88→     * When converting a text (possibly contains both tabs and spaces) to tabs, all tabs are first converted to
 89→     * spaces. Each consecutive set of spaces is converted to tabs. Sapces which are left, e.g. not enough space to
 90→     * complete a full tab, are ignored silently.
 91→     */
 92→    public fun toNormalizedIndent(text: String): String {
 93→        val indent = getTextAfterLastNewLine(text)
 94→        require(indent.matches(TABS_AND_SPACES))
 95→        return when (indentStyle) {
 96→            SPACE -> indent.replaceTabWithSpaces()
 97→            TAB -> {
 98→                "\t".repeat(indentLevelFrom(indent))
 99→                // Silently swallow spaces if not enough spaces present to convert to a tab
100→                // val spaceCount = asSpaces.length - (tabCount * tabWidth)
101→                // "\t".repeat(tabCount) + " ".repeat(spaceCount)
102→            }
103→        }
104→    }
105→
106→    private fun getTextAfterLastNewLine(text: String): String {
107→        val index = text.indexOfLast { it == '\n' }
108→        val indent =
109→            if (index == -1) {
110→                text
111→            } else {
112→                text.substring(index + 1, text.length)
113→            }
114→        return indent
115→    }
116→
117→    private fun String.replaceTabWithSpaces() = replace("\t", " ".repeat(tabWidth))
118→
119→    /**
120→     * Gets the indent level for given [text]. If [text] contains a new line, then only text after the last new line
121→     * is considered. This text may only contain spaces and tabs.
122→     *
123→     * Tabs in the [text] are first converted to spaces depending on the tab size of the indent style. For each set
124→     * of spaces that is equivalent to one tab is counted as indentation level. Space that are remaining because
125→     * they do not fill up an entire tab, are ignored silently.
126→     */
127→    public fun indentLevelFrom(text: String): Int {
128→        val indent = getTextAfterLastNewLine(text)
129→        require(indent.matches(TABS_AND_SPACES))
130→        return indent.replaceTabWithSpaces().length / tabWidth
131→    }
132→
133→    public fun containsUnexpectedIndentChar(indentText: String): Boolean = indentText.contains(unexpectedIndentChar)
134→
135→    public fun indexOfFirstUnexpectedIndentChar(indentText: String): Int = indentText.indexOfFirst { it == unexpectedIndentChar }
136→
137→    public val unexpectedIndentCharDescription: String =
138→        when (indentStyle) {
139→            SPACE -> "tab"
140→            TAB -> "space"
141→        }
142→
143→    public companion object {
144→        private val TABS_AND_SPACES = Regex("[ \t]*")
145→
146→        public val DEFAULT_INDENT_CONFIG: IndentConfig =
147→            IndentConfig(
148→                indentStyle = INDENT_STYLE_PROPERTY.defaultValue,
149→                tabWidth = INDENT_SIZE_PROPERTY.defaultValue,
150→            )
151→    }
152→}

 5→
 6→/**
 7→ * Definition of '.editorconfig' property enriched with fields required by the [com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.KtLintRuleEngine].
 8→ *
 9→ * Properties can be:
10→ * - universal `.editorconfig` properties defined
11→ *   [here](https://github.com/editorconfig/editorconfig/wiki/EditorConfig-Properties#current-universal-properties)
12→ * - universal IntelliJ IDEA properties defined
13→ *   [here](https://github.com/JetBrains/intellij-community/blob/master/platform/lang-api/src/com/intellij/psi/codeStyle/CommonCodeStyleSettings.java)
14→ * - Kotlin specific properties defined
15→ *   [here](https://github.com/JetBrains/kotlin/blob/master/idea/formatter/src/org/jetbrains/kotlin/idea/core/formatter/KotlinCodeStyleSettings.java)
16→ */
17→public data class EditorConfigProperty<T>(
18→    /**
19→     * Type of property. Could be one of default ones (see [PropertyType.STANDARD_TYPES]) or custom one.
20→     */
21→    public val type: PropertyType<T>,
22→    /**
23→     * Default value for property if it does not exist in loaded properties. This default applies to all code styles
24→     * unless the code style specific default value is set.
25→     */
26→    public val defaultValue: T,
27→    /**
28→     * Default value for property if it does not exist in loaded properties and codestyle 'ktlint_official'. When not
29→     * set, it is defaulted to [defaultValue].
30→     */
31→    public val ktlintOfficialCodeStyleDefaultValue: T = defaultValue,
32→    /**
33→     * Default value for property if it does not exist in loaded properties and codestyle 'intellij_idea'. When not
34→     * set, it is defaulted to [defaultValue].
35→     */
36→    public val intellijIdeaCodeStyleDefaultValue: T = defaultValue,
37→    /**
38→     * Default value for property if it does not exist in loaded properties and codestyle 'android_studio'. When not
39→     * set, it is defaulted to [defaultValue].
40→     */
41→    public val androidStudioCodeStyleDefaultValue: T = defaultValue,
42→    /**
43→     * If set, it maps the actual value set for the property, to another valid value for that property. See example
44→     * below where
45→     * ```kotlin
46→     * propertyMapper = { property, isAndroidCodeStyle ->
47→     *     when {
48→     *         property == null ->
49→     *             // property is not defined in ".editorconfig" file
50→     *         property.isUnset ->
51→     *             // property is defined in ".editorconfig" file with special value "unset"
52→     *         property.sourceValue == "some-string-value" ->
53→     *             // property is defined in ".editorconfig" file with a value that needs to be remapped to another
54→     *             // valid value. For example the "max_line_length" property accepts value "off" but is remapped to
55→     *             // "-1" in ktlint.
56→     *        else ->
57→     *             property.getValueAs() // or null
58→     *     }
59→     * }
60→     * ```
61→     * In case the lambda returns a null value then, the code style specific default value or the generic [defaultValue]
62→     * is used.
63→     */
64→    public val propertyMapper: ((Property?, CodeStyleValue) -> T?)? = null,
65→    /**
66→     * Custom function that represents [T] as String. Defaults to the standard `toString()` call. Override the
67→     * default implementation in case you need a different behavior than the standard `toString()` (e.g. for
68→     * collections joinToString() is more applicable).
69→     */
70→    public val propertyWriter: (T) -> String = { it.toString() },
71→    /**
72→     * Optional message to be displayed whenever the value of the property is being retrieved while it has been deprecated.
73→     */
74→    val deprecationWarning: String? = null,
75→    /**
76→     * Optional message to be displayed whenever the value of the property is being retrieved while it has been deprecated.
77→     */
78→    val deprecationError: String? = null,
79→    /**
80→     * Name of the property. A property must be named in case multiple properties are defined for the same type.
81→     * Defaults to the name of the type when not set.
82→     */
83→    public val name: String = type.name,
84→)
